title: Platform Operator Lifecycle Automation Cookbook

Summary:
This cookbook describes a standardized, automation-first approach to the platform operator lifecycle at Redwood Inference. text:
Summary:
This cookbook describes a standardized, automation-first approach to the platform operator lifecycle at Redwood Inference. It covers operator onboarding, preflight validations, automated certification and proctoring, staged change rollouts, offboarding, and continuous skills/knowledge validation. The goal is to reduce manual handoffs, compress time-to-competency, and ensure consistent operator responses during incidents and maintenance windows.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: Platform and SRE operator lifecycle activities that touch the platform operator surface (prefetch/cache operators, deployment operators, capacity operators, regional failover).
- Audience: Platform Engineering (primary), Eng-SRE, Team Leads, Hiring/People Ops (secondary).

Goals:
1. Automate repetitive operator onboarding tasks so new operators reach certified readiness within 10 business days.
2. Guarante...

title: capacity-signal-fabric-and-incident-fuse-playbook-2026
preview:
Overview

This playbook defines the "Capacity Signal Fabric" and the complementary "Incident Fuse" pattern for Redwood Inference service teams. The intent is to unify signals (SLOs, capacity telemetry, cost and model-level health) into a single, operational fabric that can drive deterministic hedging decisions, auto...
text:
Overview

This playbook defines the "Capacity Signal Fabric" and the complementary "Incident Fuse" pattern for Redwood Inference service teams. The intent is to unify signals (SLOs, capacity telemetry, cost and model-level health) into a single, operational fabric that can drive deterministic hedging decisions, automated mitigations, and incident fuse behavior when global or cross-service capacity risk is detected. The document covers signal taxonomy, thresholds, automation runbooks, incident review checklist, and recommended tooling integrations.

Scope

- Applies to all model-serving and routing services in the hosted and dedicated tiers.
- Focus areas: SLOs & error budgets, capacity planning hedges, reliability initiatives, incident fuse triggers and automated mitigations, runbooks for responders, and post-incident standards.
- Not covered: billing reconciliation, long-term contrac...

title: SDK Authentication: Resolution Order & Secret Handling (Security-Reviewed)
preview:
## Summary
This document defines the **security-reviewed** requirements for how Redwood SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go) must:
1) resolve credentials (explicit parameters vs environment variables),
2) format authentication headers,
3) present safe error messages, and
4) handle **logging/redaction** and recommended **secr...
text:
## Summary
This document defines the **security-reviewed** requirements for how Redwood SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go) must:
1) resolve credentials (explicit parameters vs environment variables),
2) format authentication headers,
3) present safe error messages, and
4) handle **logging/redaction** and recommended **secret storage** practices.

This guidance is part of the cross-language SDK parity initiative. Any deviation requires Security sign-off.

---

## Goals
- Ensure consistent, predictable credential resolution across SDKs.
- Prevent accidental secret disclosure via logs, exceptions, analytics/telemetry, stack traces, or debug tooling.
- Standardize environment variable names to reduce customer confusion and support load.

## Non-goals
- Introducing new auth mechanisms (OAuth flows, refresh tokens, mTLS, etc.).
- Changing Redwood server-side auth semantics.

---

## Supported cred...

title: Audit Logging (Customer Overview)
preview:
## Summary
Redwood Inference provides audit logs to help customers answer: **who did what, when, and from where** across the Redwood Console and API. Audit logs are designed for security monitoring and compliance evidence, and support:
- A **standard event taxonomy** (auth, access, configuration changes, administrat...
text:
## Summary
Redwood Inference provides audit logs to help customers answer: **who did what, when, and from where** across the Redwood Console and API. Audit logs are designed for security monitoring and compliance evidence, and support:
- A **standard event taxonomy** (auth, access, configuration changes, administrative actions)
- **Searchable audit log viewing** in the Redwood Console (and API access where enabled)
- **Export** to customer-controlled destinations (e.g., **S3-compatible object storage** and **SIEM ingestion**) depending on deployment mode and plan
- **Retention controls** with plan- and deployment-specific options

This page is written to be customer-shareable as an overview. For implementation specifics (exact API endpoints, regions, and configuration UI), coordinate with your Solutions Engineer.

---

## Where audit logs apply (scope)
Audit logs cover actions taken i...

title: Exception Policy: Optimize Quality Gate Overrides
preview:
## Summary
Optimize can recommend and apply cost-saving configuration changes (batching policy, prefix/KV caching settings, and quantization profiles). Because these changes can materially affect model outputs, **quality gates are mandatory** prior to production rollout.

This policy defines the **only approved path...
text:
## Summary
Optimize can recommend and apply cost-saving configuration changes (batching policy, prefix/KV caching settings, and quantization profiles). Because these changes can materially affect model outputs, **quality gates are mandatory** prior to production rollout.

This policy defines the **only approved path to override** a failing or missing quality gate, including who can approve, time bounds, required evidence, audit requirements, and required follow-up.

Overrides are intended to be **rare, time-bound, and traceable**. The default expectation is to resolve the underlying issue (regression, flakiness, or infra failure) rather than bypass gating.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Overrides for Optimize-proposed configuration changes:
 - Batching policy updates
 - Prefix/KV caching settings
 - Quantization profile changes (per model family / hardware SKU)
- Overrides that affect:...
links: ADR-0231 Quality gates required for Optimize config changes
Quality gate metrics and thresholds v1

title: Shadow Traffic: Data Retention, Redaction, and Residency Policy
preview:

This document defines Redwood Inference’s data handling policy for Shadow Traffic (a.k.a. “Shadow Experiments”) artifacts. It covers:

text:
## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s data handling policy for Shadow Traffic (a.k.a. “Shadow Experiments”) artifacts. It covers:
- What data is captured (and what is not)
- Default retention windows and deletion behavior
- Redaction/transformation rules for prompts and outputs
- Customer controls and opt-outs
- Dedicated and Private deployment configuration knobs
- Residency constraints and cross-region restrictions

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow” variants, scores outputs asynchronously, and surfaces quality metrics without changing the user response. This policy applies to data generated by:
- Shadow request mirroring (data plane)
- Shadow pair telemetry events (observability)
- Scoring/evals outputs (Applied ML / eval-harness)
- Console drilldowns (sampled examples)

## Scope and definitions
### In-scope data...

title: Artifact Signing Standard (Company-wide)
preview:
## Purpose
This standard defines Redwood Inference’s minimum requirements for cryptographic signing of release artifacts. The goal is to reduce supply-chain risk by ensuring integrity and provenance of artifacts delivered to customers or used to run customer workloads.

This document is company-wide and applies to a...
text:
## Purpose
This standard defines Redwood Inference’s minimum requirements for cryptographic signing of release artifacts. The goal is to reduce supply-chain risk by ensuring integrity and provenance of artifacts delivered to customers or used to run customer workloads.

This document is company-wide and applies to all product lines and deployment modes (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). Additional requirements may be layered on by product-specific docs, but may not weaken the controls here.

## Scope
In scope:
- What artifacts must be signed and verified
- Minimum metadata and canonicalization requirements
- Key custody and access controls
- Verification requirements (including “fail-closed” expectations)
- Audit logging and evidence expectations
- Exception / break-glass process

Out of scope:
- Full customer-managed signing root delegation (tracked as a future capability for some Private...

title: Threat Model: Offline bundle signing for Redwood Private (air-gapped installs)
preview:
## Summary
This document threat-models **offline artifact bundles** for Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem packaging) that are distributed into **air-gapped** environments. The goal is to ensure **integrity and provenance** of all artifacts required to install/upgrade Redwood Private, and to ensure the installer **fails...
text:
## Summary
This document threat-models **offline artifact bundles** for Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem packaging) that are distributed into **air-gapped** environments. The goal is to ensure **integrity and provenance** of all artifacts required to install/upgrade Redwood Private, and to ensure the installer **fails closed** when verification fails (with an explicit, audited break-glass path).

This threat model covers:
- Offline bundle tampering (manifest/artifact modification, deletion, insertion)
- Replay and downgrade attacks (reusing old but valid bundles/signatures)
- Key compromise scenarios (signing key, build keys, trust root distribution)
- Build and release pipeline threats (CI runner compromise, artifact substitution)
- Verification flow threats in the on-prem installer (time skew, partial verification)

Out of scope (initial phase): customer-signed bundles / delegated tru...

text:
## Purpose
This page defines the **recommended IAM posture** for customers deploying **Redwood Private** using the **AWS VPC Terraform modules (alpha)**. It covers:
- Which IAM roles/policies the modules **create** vs **expect**.
- **Least-privilege** recommendations for Terraform and installer operations.
- Mandatory use of **permission boundaries** (and why).
- How customer IAM integrates with `redwood-private-installer` workflows.

The intent is to reduce pilot risk (over-permissioned roles, accidental public access, unclear separation of duties) while keeping the alpha modules usable across common enterprise IAM setups.

---

## Scope and assumptions
**In scope (alpha):**
- IAM scaffolding to run Terraform modules safely in a customer AWS account.
- A dedicated **installer execution role** for `redwood-private-installer` (or an equivalent customer-run automation) with a **permissi...

## Overview
This document specifies the **audit log events** emitted for **Hosted canary rollout actions** in Redwood Console / Platform Control Plane. These events support:
- Customer governance and change control (who changed what, when, and why)
- Incident investigations and root cause analysis
- Compliance evidence (SOX/SOC2/ISO-style change management)
- SIEM ingestion/export with stable schemas and retention guarantees

This spec covers:
1) Event taxonomy (names, when emitted)
2) Standard event envelope fields
3) Rollout-specific fields and diff format
4) Retention and access expectations
5) SIEM export considerations
6) Examples

## Scope
### In scope
- Hosted routes canary rollout workflow actions (create/start/step/pause/resume/rollback)
- Approvals (human approval gates)
- Policy decision enforcement outcomes for rollout actions
- Auto-stop / auto-rollback actions triggered ...

text:
## 1. Purpose
This document defines the **canonical audit event schema v2** used across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated) and provides **mapping guidance** for common SIEM destinations in the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 delivery).

**Goals**
- Provide a stable, versioned schema for audit events suitable for: incident response, compliance evidence, and enterprise SIEM ingestion.
- Ensure a consistent set of identifiers for deduplication and correlation (event_id, request_id/trace_id).
- Ensure PII minimization and predictable field semantics across services.

**Non-goals**
- This document does not define exporter delivery semantics (see ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency).
- This document does not define retention/backfill responsibilities (see policy: retention vs export responsibilities).

---

## 2. Schema overview
Audit Event Schem...

title: Onboarding Telemetry Data Review (Security & Compliance)
preview:

- Data classification and permitted identifiers

text:
## Summary
This document records the Security & Compliance review for the onboarding telemetry used by the **Guided Onboarding + First Production Launch checklist** in the Redwood Console. It covers:
- Data classification and permitted identifiers
- PII avoidance rules and validation expectations
- Retention and deletion policy (raw vs derived)
- Regional data residency constraints (US/EU) and cross-region access
- Access controls and audit considerations

Decision outcome: **Approved** for production use under the constraints and retention controls below.

## Background
The onboarding checklist relies on event instrumentation across the API gateway/control plane and limited Console client hints. Telemetry is used to:
- Compute checklist step state (e.g., “API key created”, “first successful request”, “first streaming response”)
- Produce onboarding KPIs (TTFS/TTFP, funnel drop-off)
-...

title: RBAC v2 Permission Inventory & Classification (Canonical Catalog)
preview:

It is used to:
- Standardize **permission naming** and **sensitivity classification**.
- Map **product actions (endpoints/jobs/Console workflows)** to permissions.
- Identify **gaps** (missing checks, missing permissions, in...
text:
## Purpose
This page is the **canonical permission catalog** for Redwood Inference RBAC v2.

It is used to:
- Standardize **permission naming** and **sensitivity classification**.
- Map **product actions (endpoints/jobs/Console workflows)** to permissions.
- Identify **gaps** (missing checks, missing permissions, inconsistent error semantics).
- Provide a source of truth for **role template generation** and **CI enforcement**.

> Ownership note: Security owns the catalog format and tiering. Each domain team owns correctness for their mapped actions and coverage.

---

## Definitions
- **Permission**: A named capability that may be granted via roles and bindings.
- **Action**: A concrete operation (API endpoint, internal RPC, background job) that must be protected by a permission.
- **Resource**: The object being acted upon (org/project/environment/deployment/key/etc.).
- **Scope**: Th...

title: RBAC v2 Design Overview (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
preview:
## Summary
RBAC v2 is Redwood Inference’s fine-grained authorization model designed to support regulated enterprise workloads across Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments. This document describes RBAC v2 goals, primitives, evaluation flow, storage model, caching strategy, and rollout ...
text:
## Summary
RBAC v2 is Redwood Inference’s fine-grained authorization model designed to support regulated enterprise workloads across Hosted API, Dedicated capacity, and Private (VPC/on-prem) deployments. This document describes RBAC v2 goals, primitives, evaluation flow, storage model, caching strategy, and rollout plan.

RBAC v2 is the authorization layer. Authentication (API keys, SSO/SAML/OIDC, service identities) remains unchanged, but all security-relevant actions must map to an explicit permission and be deny-by-default.

## Goals
1. Least-privilege authorization with a canonical permission catalog covering the Redwood product surface area (API Gateway, Console, Control Plane services, Dedicated/Private admin operations).
2. Consistent enforcement and error semantics across:
 - API Gateway (edge enforcement)
 - Control Plane services (authoritative enforcement)
 - Console/admin ...
links: ADR-015 Centralized policy evaluation vs embedded
RBAC v2 migration and backwards compatibility

title: Break-glass access (RBAC v2): emergency admin procedure and controls
preview:
## Overview
Break-glass access is a controlled, time-bound mechanism to temporarily grant elevated privileges to resolve an incident, restore service, or perform a customer-authorized emergency operation when normal access paths (standard RBAC roles, delegated admin workflows, or automation) are insufficient.

This ...
text:
## Overview
Break-glass access is a controlled, time-bound mechanism to temporarily grant elevated privileges to resolve an incident, restore service, or perform a customer-authorized emergency operation when normal access paths (standard RBAC roles, delegated admin workflows, or automation) are insufficient.

This page defines:
- When break-glass is allowed vs prohibited
- The approval workflow (two-person rule) and emergency exceptions
- How time-bound elevation is implemented in RBAC v2
- Required logging, alerting, and evidence artifacts
- Post-use review requirements and follow-ups

Break-glass is designed to be compatible across **Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** deployments, with deployment-specific operational details called out below.

---

## Goals
1. **Restore or protect service quickly** during incidents while maintaining strong controls.
2. **Deny-by-default**...

title: Secure SDLC Addendum: Template Dependency & Supply Chain Policy
preview:
## Purpose
This document is a Secure SDLC policy addendum for **Redwood Quickstart Templates** ("templates"), including:
- GitHub template repositories (e.g., `redwood-quickstarts`)
- Template CLI/scaffolder output (generated projects)
- Example apps shipped alongside templates when they share the same release proce...
text:
## Purpose
This document is a Secure SDLC policy addendum for **Redwood Quickstart Templates** ("templates"), including:
- GitHub template repositories (e.g., `redwood-quickstarts`)
- Template CLI/scaffolder output (generated projects)
- Example apps shipped alongside templates when they share the same release process

Templates are intentionally copy/pasted into customer environments. That makes them a **supply-chain multiplier**: a single insecure default or compromised dependency can propagate widely. This policy sets minimum requirements for dependency pinning, vulnerability scanning, and safe handling of secrets and sample data.

## Scope
### In scope
- Template source repos maintained by Redwood Inference (Python and TypeScript initially)
- Build/test/deploy artifacts and workflows in those repos (CI, Dockerfiles, GitHub Actions)
- Sample data and configuration included in templ...

## Purpose
This document assesses security, reliability, and compliance risks introduced by the Retention Controls feature set (customer-configurable TTLs, purge semantics, legal hold, and verification) and defines required mitigations, monitoring, and residual risks.

This risk assessment is used for:

text:
# Overview
This page explains how to manually shift production inference traffic between regions/clusters by applying routing overrides in the Router/Policy Service.

# When to use
- Regional degradation or cloud incident
- GPU capacity shortage in a region/cluster
- Elevated 5xx or sustained p95 latency regression

# What this changes
- Route weights for new requests only
- Optional: disabling a route group entirely (hard failover)
- Optional: forcing routing to a specific cluster for containment

# Guardrails
- Only the incident commander or oncall lead should approve production overrides
- Overrides must be time-bounded (set an expiry)
- Always leave a rollback note in the incident channel

# Inputs
- policy_name: hosted-api-prod (primary)
- route_group: one of chat, embeddings, rerank
- selector: tier=standard|enterprise; region preference if relevant

# Procedure
1) Confirm capac...
